Welcome everyone to the Total Package Conference number three in our series. The title of today's conference is Write Better, Copy Faster, produce up to twice as many projects per year and get bigger winners more often. This is everything you want. You want to get more packages out, you want more winners so everybody wants you as a coffee writer and you're more productive so you're actually making more money on the advances on the front end in addition to all those packages becoming more of those packages becoming controlled so you're making money on the back end. With me are Clayton Makepeace. Hi Clayton. Hey, how are you doing? Good. And Wendy. That's me the redhead, I'm here. So just before we start, a little bit of feedback. We're back up with our email system so you can begin sending email questions which we will get assuming there's no more server problems but we think we're back up and running so you can email your questions as always to feedback at MakepeaceTotalPackage.com. Let's dive into the beginning of Write Better, Copy Faster and produce up to twice as many projects per year. The first bullet we found from experience, our best way to do this is go down what we promised you in the promotion and if you look at the promotion to which brought you to this call, our first bullet was five secrets for finishing each project in half the time it takes you now. So obviously a copywriter, royalties aside, a copywriter has the same problem. If you're a copywriter and you work, let's say you don't have employees or you don't farm out to other copywriters but you work for yourself, you're a freelance writer, we have the same problem a dentist has. Dentists have this expression, Clayton, I don't know if you've ever heard this one. If you're not drilling and filling, you're not billing. Which means highly paid as they are, they're limited by their own labor. If they're not in the dental office, filling cavities, they don't make money. And we on the front end at least have the same thing. Obviously we have income beyond that with royalties and mailing fees but on the front end, if you're getting paid a flat via five or $10,000 or if you write a package that doesn't pull a royalty and you just get your upfront money, we have the same thing. If you're getting $1,000 a project and you can do one a week, you make $1,000 a week. But if you can do two a week, if you can do half the time it takes you now, you can do $2,000 a week and you suddenly can double your income from $50,000 to $100,000 a year. So I want to know how it takes, how you, I have my own methods but I want to know, Clayton, how can people listening to this call do projects twice as fast or get them done in half the time? What are your methods? Well, again, I just want to, I want us to step back from what we're doing here for a second and think about copywriting as what it really is, which is a business. This is, it's fun, it's easy, it gives you freedom but first and foremost it's a business, you're doing it for money. And how do you measure your income? If someone asks you how much money you make, you say, well, I make $100,000 a year. There are two components to that, the amount that you make and how long that you take to make it. The more projects that you do in a year and can build for, the more money you'll make. And so it's very important to understand that when projects are moved into next year, that money's lost forever to this year. This new income is reduced by exactly that amount. So the number of packages that you can create in a year is absolutely crucial in order to maximize your income. A lot of people really don't think about that and they think that, you know, all right, so I'll make the money on January 15th instead of on December 15th. But what you've just done is reduce your income by that amount. And that's really important for clients to know too. A lot of what I do in marketing consultation with my clients is speeding the process so that we get more mailings per year. You know, with copywriters, everything is bigger winners more often. With clients, it's bigger winners more frequently during the year so that you get and bigger mailings more often. And so that's an absolute key to this. In our last call, we talked about, actually we got a little ahead of ourselves. We talked a little bit about some of the things that a copywriter can do to finish projects quickly. We talked about outsourcing research or getting the client to provide the research for you. We talked about having, you know, compartmentalizing each project and maybe you have a typing service. Do the long typing work. Like me, you do work for boardroom and there are a lot of proof elements that you're copying directly out of a book or some other product. A typing service or, you know, I of course have a staff and I have a girl who is just absolutely a whiz on the keyboard and she types very quickly and saves me days on each boardroom project by just typing the highlighted copy that I've selected for my proof element. Another thing to look for is to ask your client when you're asking for background research and so forth to ask for digital documents. I actually have a major, major client that has never converted their back newsletter issues into PDFs. PDFs are great because in the little task bar at the top of the pane there's something that says select and if you push that button you can scroll through a PDF and copy any copy out of the PDF and paste it into your Word document that you want to and that can save you enormous amounts of time. Later on we're going to be talking about outlines, starter outlines for packages and how they can save you time. Once you have one of these outlines that you're comfortable with and you have it kind of laid out in a Word document then it's very easy to go through and very quick to go through if you're doing work for publishers particularly to go through back issues or whatever and copy directly out of the PDF or another Word document into your outline and it can save you hours on every job. Now let me issue a warning to the listeners. If you do this even if it's the publisher's own material you have to acknowledge the source. I put in a legend at the top of my copyright or the beginning of my copyright I say sources. Here's where I basically took all the research and I have symbols so it'll be FA equals federal aviation report. The client has a report called the federal aviation report and I lift half of page 2 and put it in a 20 page promotion. I put in squiggly brackets the key code FA so they know where I took it. Even if it's their material they're going to ask you. I've actually been asked when I didn't do this, I've had clients ask me where did you get this and I'll say it was in the bunch of stuff you sent me and they said we need to know where. Yeah, I just had that with Agora and actually what I tend to do is when I copy out of something I'll paste it into my outline but then I'll also paste it into a research document that I'm creating. I tend to go through newsletters for example in orders so I'll say on my research document I'll say January 2003 and that's the issued date and then I'll copy whatever I want to use out of that, paste it into the research document then paste it into my outline. When I'm finished if anybody wants to come back to me and say yeah well where did you get this number? This actually happened with a major client a week ago, it was Agora. They were all worried because there were dozens of references that they didn't know where I got them from and every single one of them was from their own newsletters. All I had to do was find a keyword in my research document and I was able to tell them what issue and even what page the information was from. The being able to paste into an outline will beets the blank page blues for you and if you're not feeling creative and have no idea which direction you're going to take with the overall, with the organization of the copy the outline will save you time also by just pasting this stuff in it does two things. One gives you content immediately you can edit. Secondly you'll be speaking in the editor's own voice so it'll pick up the richness of his own writing style and take on more of his tone. That's terrific. Then we talked about the typing and also the final one really was office chores. I tell you what Wendy just went to work for me full time for the first time in our 12 years together. She has come to work full time and she's taken over the scheduling, the bookkeeping, the invoicing, phone chores and everything that doesn't have to do with actually writing copy for me. Immediately this is now a 50 hour week job for Wendy and I have to wonder how in the world I ever did it all. The thing is when you're not writing copy you're not making money and when you're not making money you're spending it because you have ongoing even if you're not taking your wallet out you have ongoing overhead, ongoing expenses. You're always in one of two modes you're either making it or you're spending it. When you're answering the phone or doing scheduling or calling clients that you're going to be working for in a couple of months and juggling schedules or keeping the books or paying the taxes or doing the invoicing or any of those other extraneous jobs anything that is an actual copywriting you're spending money and many of us have spouses that are significant others that can help with those tasks and we'd be happy to do it. As you move along and you start establishing a decent income from copywriting you need to be looking at opportunity costs all the time. Sure a bookkeeper to handle the bookkeeping and invoicing may cost you a few hundred dollars a month if you hire one as not hire one but engage a professional service. It may cost you a few hundred dollars a month but if you're a copywriter and you have the capacity to earn a half a million dollars a year like Carline does or many others of do then you have to figure out that every hour that you're spending doing that you're losing a substantial amount of money. I just let me digress for a second. One of the key things that I did early on was that I sat down and I figured out how much I wanted to make per year and then how much that was per hour. That immediately gave me a measuring stick that I could use to decide which tasks were important enough for me to do myself and which ones were cheaper to pay someone else to do. So you figure forty hours a week times fifty two weeks and you divide the amount of money that you want to make per hour by that. The amount of money you want to make in a year you divide it and it tells you oh well I'm worth a thousand dollars an hour. That's my goal. So if you can find somebody to do one of these tasks for you for twenty bucks it's a no-brainer. And I'm going to stop on this for a minute. I tell everyone and I'm giving a talk to a local New Jersey group next week called NJ Creatives where they're writers and graphic designers and you know to talk on how they can double or triple their income. The first thing I tell them is they have to establish a dollar value per hour for their time and if you meet people almost no one that you meet actually does this. And therefore if you don't have a dollar value for your time the value of that time in your mind is zero. You'll always do other things. I give you an example. I was in the grocery store the other day on the way home picking something up and I was picking up some butter and this older gentleman next to me says you should put that back and I said what he says tomorrow it will be a dollar cheaper. And I smiled at him and I picked the butter up and put it in my cart and he looked at me like I was nuts. He doesn't get the value of time. He thinks I'm losing a dollar. And people do that all the time. They sure do. So if we look at how you can be more productive. First tip you gave us is outsource the research. You do this I do this. When I start a project I make a list of the research questions and the client hasn't provided it I give it to my freelance internet researcher. Step number two you say have someone else do the guarantee the response device and other copy. Yeah I'd like to I kind of skipped over that one. I'm just tired of you know a guy who does editorial work. He has no sales background at all. And yet you know he's perfectly equipped to go through past mail packages the client has done and put together and just copy out the guarantee the response device or institutional copy biographies you know the company history those kinds of things. Now for me I was able you know I have a full time guy who's helping me with editorial his name is Steven Banks by the way and you all are going to be meeting him in the total package in the weeks ahead he's really excellent and he's also getting quite an education. And but you know even if it's a typing service you know do those things. I'm sorry go ahead Bob. So you have someone else do the stuff that is a waste of your time to do. You know that someone else who makes much less money than you make or somewhat less money than you make can do just as well and just as easily. Step three you talked about asking for digital documents you know it used to be where people client would send you a big FedEx and they say can you email me the PDFs. Can you email me the premiums as a word document. You can take can you email me the issues or give me website access to the issues. And a lot of the times you can paste cut and paste not only text but images if you want to use a graph for example. Absolutely. Step four you said you know when typing is needed have someone else do it. I talked about last time we like optical scanning you do have to proof it carefully but it saves a lot of time. So my assistant my secretary has an optical scanner with the OCR software and we scan stuff where we don't have an electronic file. And number five you said you know you hired Wendy and other people to do to run the office and you mentioned things like bookkeeping, phoning, scheduling, making your schedule and so forth and I've also done that for years and the way I express that to people said I have not been to the post office in 17 years. Why shouldn't you go to the post office or the coffee shop when you can hire a high school kid to do that or college kid to do that for $10 an hour. You know I think as a copywriter you actually goofing off is actually a better use of your time than mowing the lawn unless you enjoy mowing the lawn and you find it a relaxing exercise and you actually do some thinking while you're doing it. But I really find that you know doing the chores around the house and those other things that take my time and that I don't enjoy those kinds of chores are not really constructive but time off can be very constructive. I did an article in the total package not too long ago the human brain and owner's manual and in that we talk about how your brain works and how when your conscious mind is not fully engaged it allows with a particular subject you know like for example you're writing a package. Any time that you're awake or asleep you're away from that work. Your mind is still chewing on it. You may not be thinking about it but it's still chewing on it and you know writing my Harley going down to the shooting range just doing things where I'm goofing off and freeing my mind for an hour or two tends to really get my juices flowing again and I get a completely different look at it when I get back. So if there are chores that you enjoy doing and that you find kind of mindless and you don't mind doing the chore itself then fine. But if you're really trying to maximize your time I think it's important to compartmentalize what you do for a living and assign tasks to people who can do things cheaper or better in many cases and you can. So it's okay if you want to stuff envelopes to stuff them but make that a deliberate choice that you're doing because you want to and you find that therapeutic. Don't do it because you have to and do that instead of doing something where you could get paid for it. Right. Okay so one way to make downtime productive is to do if you're going to do any kind of non-creative task do it sort of a way to let your mind percolate the ideas you're working on. You know for me it happens to be I have a gardener and a lawn service but raking leaves in the fall serves that purpose for me for you at shooting and riding a motorcycle. What else do you do to make downtime productive? Well it's simple. For me this works great. When I learned when I heard that this whole theory about aptitudes it really helped me a lot because understanding that you have both the accounting aptitude and the creative aptitude and when you're feeling like oh my god if I have to proofread another word I'm just going to die you know what's happening there is that your accounting aptitude your detail oriented aptitude your left brain is exhausted. It's had all it can take. The brain is like any muscle in the body. It needs exercise but at some point it hits the wall. Well that's what happens with both of these aptitudes. And the same thing happens with the creative. You're sitting there you're working on headlines you're doing all kinds of creative tasks associated with copy you hit the wall you know and all of a sudden you know you just feel like your brain is mush. You'd be amazed if at those times if you turn to an accounting oriented task or detail oriented left brain task just even for a few minutes that you can come back then to the creative task later on and you'll be rejuvenated you'll have more energy and doing that throughout the day allows me to put in when I want to 17 hour days 18 hour days go to bed get six hour sleep get up do it all over again and especially in starting you know the total package and the holding company for that is the profit center you don't hear much about that but that's the company that owns the total package and starting this company over the last year is required that I do twice the work that I normally would do I have my normal clients and then I'm also building this up to help the business owners marketing execs and copywriters so it's required me to do work two days every day and having that approach being able to shift back and forth between the right and left brain tasks allows me to put in those kinds of hours even at the ripe old age of 53 without without losing steam during the day I talk to copywriters all the time Arthur Johnson is one of the very top writers in the country right now is interviewed in our next issue and you of course seen the other interviews we've done and there's a recurring theme that that most of these guys say they can only write for five hours a day and that's you know I can't write for more than five hours a day if I you know if I sat down and I just said well you know that's what I'm going to do for the next you know for as long as I can I'd play out probably around five hours but if you do three or four hours of creative writing in three or four hours of research or sorting tasks or outlining tasks or other things that are more less creative more more left brain then you can extend the amount of time you can stay at work and therefore get more done another way to do it we talked about in the total package as well but that's and I would strongly suggest that anybody who hasn't read psycho cybernetics go to Amazon.com and get it it's it's a wonderful book it's been around forever it's updated all the time but psycho cybernetics is basically the study of how the brain works and it was written by a guy who happens to be a plastic surgeon who found out that when he went into as part of his charity work went into prisons and performed cosmetic surgery on prisoners that were deformed from birth in some way they had hair lifts or whatever plus pallets he gave them a better look that the recidivism the recidivism rate among those inmates who were now comfortable with the face that they showed to the world was a tiny fraction of what of what it was for the rest of the prison population and that observation made this guy a passionate observer of the functioning of the brain and how it works and then in that book he talks about how your conscious mind is is basically the processing center and the subconscious mind is the storage center and every experience you have comes in through the conscious mind is and is consciously experienced but it's also recorded in the subconscious and at night when you're asleep every stimulus every thought everything that happens to you during the day everything is sorted by the subconscious mind and connections are made between that event or that stimulus and something else that's happened in your life and that's what dreams are dreams are where the subconscious is busy doing that work and making connections between the things that happen that day the feelings that you had everything with things that had happened in the past and and and sometimes that work is observed by the conscious mind in your sleep just you know just enough of a get through that it creates a dream and some of them can be quite vivid and a lot of them make no sense at all because your subconscious is just trying all of these connections but anyway so the very act of sleeping I say all the time when you're a copywriter you're a copywriter 24 hours a day well you are you know if you're mowing the lawn you're going to be chewing on it if you're taking a shower in the morning either consciously or subconsciously you'll be chewing on it and when you're asleep at night your subconscious is making those connections for you and the famous story of Tom Edison who would be working on a problem he couldn't solve so he'd take a nap in his office and when he woke up the answer would be there for him so downtime sleeping is definitely downtime if you're talking about the eight hours you get at night but also napping if it doesn't interrupt with you know getting your good solid hours eight hours a night there's also another way to to re to to accelerate the writing process when you come back to the actual work you're going to be you're going to see it with new eyes because you'll have all those new connections that have been created for you by your subconscious another way to do it is I make a point that when I'm goofing off maybe I'm antique shopping with Wendy or maybe we're out on our motorcycles and sitting at a biker bar or something and talking to people wherever I am whatever I'm doing I constantly make a point and it takes it takes practice at first but if you kind of if you just you know try to do it it becomes habit fairly quickly is to every hour or so or several times of the day just to get back in touch with your creative muse just kind of yeah you're out you're not working but just think about the project think about an unsolved problem think about a headline challenge think about you know what is it about that project that's bothering me where you know I you you'll you'll have a feeling when you're writing that I call it having a stone in my shoe it's there's just something not right you know the copy is great the products great the benefits are there but there's just something not right and if you sit there and focus on it try to figure out what's right nine times out of ten you just get frustrated but once you get away from the work and you allow your mind not to obsess on that for a while and then just come back to the work mentally and think about it from time to time you'll be surprised how much will come to you that way and that's why I think for me I've had some of my best ideas in the shower I'm not thinking about work yet I'm just barely awake but I've had time away from the work and I'm looking forward to the day ahead and I think all of a sudden I think oh yeah I gotta solve that problem what is wrong with that and bang you know there's the answer and you rush upstairs to the computer to write it down how do you make sure you don't forget that well I tend not to forget those kinds of things but I have no advice for what to do in this shower to remember something but make a mental note when you get out make a note yeah my wife criticizes me the one thing I have and I have because I agree with you you're a copywriter 24 hours a day but she doesn't get this and she criticizes that I always walk around I don't know if you noticed it when you when you sit with me I have a little CVS notepad and a pen always in my front shirt pocket in fact I don't wear t-shirts because I always want to wear a shirt where I can have a pocket for a pad and I am never without a pad mm-hmm I think that's smart other people buy these little the my voice recorder yeah the micro digital voice recorders yeah and it's they're the smaller than a fountain pen and you know you can hack my BMW had a memo function where you just push a button and you can dictate while you're driving I mean that was nice so the and then the other thing is and it really ties back in the Tom Edison thing keep keep a tablet by your bedside to carry one around with you like Bob does if that helps you or some kind of a recorder find a way to keep notes but some of the some of the best impressions you'll get are in those first waking hours and minutes in the morning if it if you're Wendy is the few you waking hours of the morning she's a little slower in the morning because I'm better at I'm better in the morning she's better at night but the that time is really important because that's where you start getting the feedback from all the work your subconscious did at night make sense any other things we can think of for I mean that's that these are good suggestions for getting the creativity flowing and using the downtime or have we have we covered I think that's it use both aptitudes when one gives out use the other use the sleep the trick the Edison sleep trick stay in touch with your muse even when you're taking time off think you know cast your mind back at the project and then keep away to keep a record of impressions or thoughts they come to you when you're not engaged with the work no pad in the pocket or digital recorder yep absolutely now the next item we promised is we talk about the six types of writers block and how to over how to overcome each I had one fairly recently and and you've called you've called this in your writings intimidation or in my case it was a package for a to product area that I haven't written about it was financial but it wasn't stocks which I've done a lot of it was another type of investment that I hadn't done and I just kind of put it off because I was intimidated it was just you know it just seemed too difficult so what are the what are the six types of writers block that you see the writers you work with encounter and and can we get past them no absolutely you can get past them I think that the the first key though is to recognize what what is behind the writers block and the first the first one you just mentioned for example intimidation there there are a lot of different kinds of intimidation I'm working with young writers I'm working with Andrew over in Netherlands I'm working with Daniel up in Canada several others and one of the downsides of this whole doing the total package for me is that it kind of creates an aura people see you on the net they see your picture they read your stuff and then when they start writing for you or working with you they feel pretty intimidated it's like it's like you know in the old days the copycups I had would just say oh yeah Clayton's writer he's got a lot of winners you know and I've heard of him and I'd love to say have him enter me now though that there's this whole kind of I'm sorry anybody opposed to our rated profanity stop your ears right now but it's bullshit you know it's just bullshit all of this celebrity stuff is just but it's a real emotion can I tell you something it's a real emotion but the sooner you can cut through it and get rid of it realize it's not about me it's not about you it's about the work then the sooner you can get rid of that kind of intimidation just and be successful you know if you're thinking about pleasing the senior copywriter who's mentoring you or if you're thinking about anything other than the product and the customer then it's sapping your energies and it's it's counterproductive and so that kind of intimidation is a real problem you're gonna say something Bob yeah well give me an example for any time you or a writer you work with we're intimidated when starting a direct mail project and and and how you handled it although I can't imagine you ever being intimidated well I'm okay well Andrew I'm not gonna actually read the email I sent you but just two weeks ago a young writer showed this shows great promise and who I knew could do the work or would have gotten the assignment in the first place began work for me now I sent him an email said you're hired you know we worked out of financial arrangement right and and a deadline he then spent the next three days telling everyone he knew that he was working with Clayton make these right he then you know went through emotional swings from from excitement to oh my god you know how you know what's gonna happen when when Clayton sees my first draft right now he's probably smiling and nodding his head he went he went through this whole emotional roller coaster right this is great I'm gonna be a millionaire to oh my god well you know maybe not because Clayton's not paying me a million dollars on my first project so a little disappointment that he's gonna have to work his way up probably a lot of trepidation over showing his first draft to me and having me eviscerated worried about me kind of stomping his dream for him by creating his copy so he was dealing with all this emotional baggage instead of just saying look what is the product what does it do for the customer who is the customer how does he feel about those benefits and let me focus on that so all of that other stuff was just distracting him so I sent him an email after I saw his first rough and I just said Andrew get over yourself this isn't about you it's not about me you know I promise I'll be gentle with my crits you don't have to worry about I don't expect final copy on the first rough draft just relax take a deep breath and do the work so you know when you find yourself blocked sometimes whenever you find yourself blocked ask why am I blocked the answer is intimidation then say oh I'm intimidated okay cool well you know that that's an invalid that's an invalid emotion you may be feeling it but it's not logical and it's counterproductive because the the outcome if you produce kick butt copy the outcome is going to be everything that you're dreaming about now in terms of income freedom all of the other things the praise from your clients all of those so so recognize what it is and then deal with whether whether or not it's valid it's never valid to be intimidated I don't care who you're talking to you know they put their pants on one one like it at time it's really just about the work another kind of intimidation happened to me years ago when Jay Abraham started his first newsletter for Phillips and Jay asked for me to write his launch package for him and I said I would Jay and I had a long history together we'd known each other for years we'd never worked together before and so I said sure all right I'll write the promotion for you and next thing I know a UPS truck pulls up and unloaded I think it was 32 beacons box full of everything this guy had ever written in his entire career and if you know Jay this is a very believable story this this I've talked to several other writers who worked with him since they say that he did the same thing to them and so you know I've I've clients tend not to really enforce deadlines on me a lot they'd rather have the quality than then you know the the split second deadline thing and so I always knew every morning when I got up I knew that you know the client's not going to know if I just don't start on this today I mean I was looking at a garage full of stuff just sorting through it and trying to figure out what was what was pertinent what wasn't was a major challenge and and and when I wasn't looking forward to and so day after day I found an excuse to put it off one more day I have a one of my copycups who is quite successful has the same problem when a new project arrives whether it's a six or twelve-inch stack of research in a start kit or a box of stuff he does the same thing he says just I just can't deal with this today it's too big a project you know it's it's the the journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step thing he's looking at the journey of a thousand miles and thinking oh my god there's just too much work to be done here he should be looking at well all I have to do is cut the tape on the box and then all I have to do okay I've done that now all I have to do is take that first piece of paper out and look at it and I can do that and if I get that done by noon today I'll be a happy guy and now we're getting kind of into the metal tricks that I use on myself I do that all the time and we'll talk about that more in a minute but but this whole idea of you know I don't have to write a package today all I have to do is open the box and sort through the paper and maybe put him into files or something like that yeah and then maybe do a little scanning and then tonight when I go to bed my subconscious will sort things out a little bit I'll be more on top of it tomorrow and then then tomorrow what I'm going to do is I'm going to start going through and highlighting things that I think I could use you know this is this is a longer copy especially this is a weeks long process of actually producing something you want to show the clients so you know understand that if you're feeling blocked and you just can't get into it it could be that kind of intimidation just being intimidated by the amount of work that lays ahead and your solution is what the psychologist called for educating children I don't know if you've heard this called chunking if the kids is I can't do the homework you say well you know why don't you just do these five problems yeah and then one okay when you're done with that you do these this page three and then when you do that just do this worksheet tonight you chunk it and that makes it so much easier I have a child who has ADD and that's what we do with him and it you know if it can help you it can help anyone get through you know this thing of being overwhelmed absolutely the great technique so the cause number one is intimidation of writer's block and you can chunk things and then the other thing is you said you know if you're intimidated focus on the work not who it is I mean I've certainly felt intimidated you mentioned people intimidated working with you on a few times I've worked with celebrities or people I thought were really much smarter than I am I also have been intimidated and that was great advice which I didn't have it's forget about them forget about you yeah just do the work you know you know you're you say you have a child who's ADD and we have a son who had some issues early on too and part of the chunking process is kind of this it's very similar to what we were talking about compartmentalizing you know you compartmentalize the different parts of a task in order to identify which ones you can do better than anyone and which ones will reward you and which ones would be better to delegate but there's another part of that too and it's thinking as a copywriter thinking through the process of writing your your sales message what what is the most effective way to begin what's the most effective second step what's the third step and so forth once once you said once you said okay well the first thing that I have to do is I have to read everything I can about this guy's product okay you go okay fine you know I can make a nice reading deck and grab a briefcase check a bunch of this stuff in there go down to Barnes and Noble grab myself a latte and sit there and read for a few hours that's fine that's great that's not intimidating that's fun but in order to chunk you first have to have sequencing you first have to have sequencing you know what's first what's second what's third what's fourth and then then within each one of those steps you can you can even place and dice it more finely if you need to if those tasks take no longer periods of time so sequencing chunking that kind of stuff really helps with copywriters too because frankly we're all IDD can I interject something just because this reminds me a lot of Carleen's interview where she talked a lot about the blank page syndrome and she had a lot of great advice about how to to break out the copy in different sections and you know just fill in the reply page now you're you know one page less that you have to to fill in and that was very I thought very helpful so people could refer back to that issue it was only like two issues ago I believe I think that's smart and and don't assume that the best way to proceed is to begin a beginning I know people who get blocked by writing a headline for days you know mm-hmm I think that's nuts yeah you said you said just forget the headline put in a placeholder forget it if the best way for you to get a grasp on the product is to write the offer and the response device first do it you know it at least once you've done that you know what you're selling if the best way for you to begin is to write the the copy about the free gift that the person gets or the series of free gifts do that first you know for if you're writing a section where you're making a substantial argument about an investment and you're at the point where you don't understand it and you know what to go on to but you can't get past it right blah blah blah and go on mm-hmm and you fill it in later time yeah yeah yeah exactly so so we know how to get then one cause of writers block is that we get intimidated and we shouldn't the next cause is you had you told me is procrastination yeah I'm in favor procrastination you know you are I'm against it I think it's but tell me why it's one of my favorite things I like it too but you're gonna pay for it not no you don't think so no I'll do it right yeah not if you do it right the key to procrastination is picking the times to procrastinate mm-hmm we all love doing procrastination because what happens is you know you're thinking about this big thing that you have to do and then when you when you give yourself permission to put it off you feel so free and so wonderful and like they've just lifted a whole burden off your shoulders and like the whole day is a blank slate before in front of you and then you can do anything you want to do with it so it's too addictive and too wonderful a feeling to to it's like weight loss there's no amount of willpower that's going to make you lose weight you know there's no amount of willpower that's gonna make you stop procrastinating everyone's gonna do it the key is to do it at it at intelligent times mm-hmm what I do with create call it creative procrastination okay if you're judicious about it I was talking to a writer who was having these problems getting started called it writer's block what it was was there were some other things going on in his life that were pretty exciting and pretty fun and he just couldn't bring himself to to get away from those things and focus on the work at hand and and the problem was with this guy that there was always something going on in his life that he would rather do okay and so he was always weeks late with every job and so what I told him was just pick your time say I will never procrastinate until I have read the assignment okay right after after I've read the assignment I'm going to allow myself to put it to take a day off you know or to or to reward myself in some other way so you choose the times procrastinate so choose your times now what's happening now what's happening is you've read the research so now you go and you take your day off what have we just talked about you're a writer 24-7 mm-hmm so you're taking your day off and you're doing things but you've loaded your brain up with everything about this project and you've done you've done some thinking you're subconscious has done it sorting now you're doing your procrastination you're not really procrastinating you're working on the job even though you're doing something fun so I love procrastination I love to do it I don't do it enough and but the key is timing just you know pick your times so you can get enough loaded up into your brain so that when you're doing other things and enjoying a beautiful fall day like it is here now your brain is still chewing on the project okay so overcoming riders block getting past intimidation number one creative procrastination number two but let's talk about number three which I think is related you said it is a beautiful full day so don't you get distracted and you know what happens when you when you just say you look at the window and say I don't I don't want to do this should you just hang it up for the day or should you keep going well sometimes you know the least productive time that you're going to have is when you decide you know I just have to cut this out that's when you're going to be getting the blank page syndrome big time you know when when you act you know you say I just have to get this out I just have to spend this time that's the worst that's the worst because your output is going to stink you're not going to be creative you know there are times there are times when you just do have to hang it up Bob I mean I I do as you as you move on through your life and you do this for longer and longer periods of time though I think you'll find that you know that happens less and less often it certainly does for me but every once in a while I have to give myself permission to just allow the distraction to take over if you're sitting there and you're working and there's a rock band practicing next door you know what's the point of even sitting there right right so so you you don't need that kind of frustration so the smart thing to do is to is to well with me I feel guilty if I'm not working it's just me I guess I've made an addition I've been in business for myself 33 years and we have what's called the Puritan work ethic yeah I guess so and you know every day I know what I do provides for my family very directly there's no illusion that it's coming through a paycheck or through you know it'll always be there whether I produce or not I know the quality and quantity of work I do is going to directly you know there's a direct link between the quality and quantity of work I do and the amount of money that that I'll have to to lavish on my family and so if I take time off for any reason for for any selfish reason I feel guilty and it's taken me an awful long time to just learn to give myself permission to do that to understand that that that kind of thing you know if you're distracted it's not your fault you've done everything you can to make it work it's just not working today then yeah just go for it well I'm like you I feel guilty if I'm not working I had one that happened yesterday I'd given a speech in Washington at the DMA in the morning and I was done real early as an 830 speech done at 1030 got right in the car came back here I said oh I have plenty of time to work I don't know why but the five hour drive just kind of tired me maybe because I ate Popeye's chicken on the way back and that's no good for me but I was so tired and I sat here for two hours and I didn't do anything what do you do when you when you're not working because you just you you're sick or you're tired you didn't sleep well what do you do then you just you just is that you can't get past that you just give it up well I think I think when it comes to energy energy is creativity creativity is energy and I think it's important to be pre-emptive when it comes to energy that Popeye's chicken cost you some money yeah but it's worth it well you everything in life is a transaction yes it is it's a trade-off yeah and and you you made a transaction and what do you make a couple thousand dollars an hour and you lost four hours work so that chicken cost you eight grand yeah but it was the spicy so it was worth it yeah and I had to write some beans and if that's the case then then cool you know if you try you you made a good you made a you're a good consumer you spent your money wisely and you're a satisfied yeah of course now you're making me feel stupid about it but we get your point so you have and that's good what you said though energy is creativity if you are tired or sick you can't speak I don't think you can be creative without high energy right and and I think the only time that I what I try to do and again I hate rules and I tend not to be real good following them but what I try very hard to do is to understand that energy is the the amount of energy you're going to have and therefore the amount of creativity you're going to have is directly related to other choices that you make in your life and and diet is one of them and you know if you're eating junk if you're eating a lot of fried foods if you're not taking supplements if you're not getting the nutrition you need an exercise if you're not getting exercise if you're going out in the evenings and getting hammered and coming to work with a hangover you know you're you're you're basically sabotaging yourself you're putting yourself in a position where when you're at your desk the next day you're not going to be creative because you have no energy and so you have to understand the 24-7 thing again here you know when you're working out you're being a copywriter you the the end result of that workout is going to be greater energy right that means greater creativity when you're making good food choices the same is true right when you're taking your supplements making sure that you're you're well nourished you're you're you're being a good copywriter and when you're deciding if you're going to drink to drink only on the weekends you're being a good copywriter or drink only drinking before you are going to have to work and when I was younger I could drink every night I was just fine in the morning right now I find if I drink on the weekend it takes me one or even two days to get back up to speed so you know recognize objectively you know no excuses the choice you make affects the amount of energy you have and and so when you're making a choice that's going to sap energy in the days ahead recognize that you're spending your own money you're losing productivity you'll produce fewer packages this year you'll make less money this year and so you know keep that in mind when you're making those choices that's absolutely true now another problem I've had that has stopped me sometimes from getting out as much as I want to you've used the term to describe you should call it postpartum depression for copywriters yeah what do you mean by that I know what you mean but yeah um well that's I just yesterday I delivered this project that has been plaguing me for about three months and I've had a lot of interruptions I couldn't get my mind around the product and the interruptions that we've had have been major you know we moved into new offices we're hiring employees we've got had business trips with all this kind of stuff and and I had a lot of other things going for this client so I didn't feel particularly pressured to to get it done really quickly and so I had the luxury of time and and I'm sure there was some not much but maybe a little bit of procrastination in there but anyway yesterday at long last I got the damn thing off my desk and I emailed it to the client and I felt and I do every time I deliver one of these things I felt like I just had a baby Wendy gets mad at me when I say that because obviously you know you and I don't really know what it's like to have a baby she's executive in charge of having babies at our house and she's actually done it a couple of times and doesn't think that you know what I do in a month or two of my time can compare to what it took her nine months to produce and I I agree with her objectively but I still feel like I've had a baby um and and so at that moment you know the last thing you want to do is to dive right into another project and I frankly don't have a solution for that one other than take the day off uh carline talks about this and I think it's wonderful when she delivers a package or when she gets a royalty check that's an automatic day off absolutely automatic day off next day there's no way she's working and uh not only is she not working she's going to go out and spend money she's going to go buy something for herself and I think you know for the obvious reasons I think that's a wonderful idea you know it's motivating it's rewarding we all need we all need short term um rewards as well as long term uh in my business where I do a lot of direct mail I'll write a package in February uh it'll be uh finalized in March it'll get through graphics in April it'll be tested in May it'll do its first roll out in July um I won't see a royalty on that package probably till August okay so the work I'm doing in February the real reward for that work isn't until August now how do you stay motivated about that you know you have to find little reward steps along the way you know if I get this done then I'm going to allow myself that and and the and setting those times up in the progress of the job I think is absolutely crucial you know when I get the first rough the first complete rough done not even something I'm going to show my client I'm going to do this for myself you know and um it's important to make sure the rewards aren't things like that are going to actually slow the process like you know I'm going to I'm going to go out and get hammered or I'm going to take a trip to the Caribbean or something the unit is way out of way out of whack or way out of perspective but um you have something short term to look forward to so you need the baby steps as well as the big steps you're looking for the big reward long term but in the short term reward yourself well when you deliver a package that's definitely one of those times and finding something that you love and saying I'm not going to do that until I finish this package um but when I do finish it I'm giving myself permission to take a day or or you know a three-day weekend or whatever and and do this great thing so um anyway let me that's a great idea no that is let me hit a let me stop for a second and give our listeners just want to say one more thing here about this whole thing of of uh writer's block what one of the strange situations that you're going to find yourself in is a writer is you sit down at you you go down to your desk in the morning right and it's time to work and you're broke and you've got a month's worth of bills that are due and you have no money and now you're supposed to be creative while you're worrying the whole time in the back of your mind about how you're gonna how you're gonna put food on the table for the next two or three weeks I went through this all the time when I was starting out how do you focus on the work when the phone's ringing and it's a bill collector how do you focus on the work when when you know maybe the bill collectors aren't calling but you know in a week or two you got a rent payment or a mortgage payment too and you don't have the cash how do you do that or what forget the creditors when you have a parent or spouse or significant other who didn't maybe approve of you doing this is saying I don't see any money coming in you're going to get a real job soon and give this up yeah that's the worst pressure that is and uh and frankly I don't know how to get over that one I the um the spouse thing not being fully on board I had an email from a couple where the the the man really wanted to get into this copywriting thing and was passionate about it and his wife wasn't supporting him and she was telling him that everyone that tells you that copywriting is a good profession they're just trying to sell you something and you know it's just a big scam and a big con game and so she was extremely negative and and at the same time he was extremely positive and I just I I said I told him you know I don't want to talk to you I want to talk to the two of you because frankly if your spouse isn't on board this isn't going to work you know and and so I replied to the two of them and I just basically told them that this is real we are making excellent money as copywriters not as people who teach copywriting but we're in the teaching isn't has not made me a penny it's cost me $150,000 the last 12 months setting this whole thing up the the the money that I've made has been 100% from royalties from clients and and I said so it is real and I can introduce you to many other people but many other successful writers who can show you it's real but don't do it if if if you can't support each other in it because it is starting a new business and it's always tough at the beginning when you start a new business and so that's the only advice I have there but I do have some other advice and I gave it to my son-in-law who decided to become a direct response graphic artist and that advice is no matter what pay yourself first when you get a check-in sock set a number and 10% is the absolute minimum but set a number of that money that some percent that you were going to sock away and that you are not going to touch no matter what the only time you get to touch that money is if you come up up on a dry spell and it could be you know there are a lot of reasons this business goes through dry spells the financial markets may may have gotten very mature or taken a lot of hits and people aren't buying financial products right now and that could go on I've seen it gone on before for a month or two more recently it went on for a year a little over a year before it finally started picking up again during those dry spells you've got to have reserves you have to have money set aside or you're going to be sitting there staring at at major financial problems and that's going to be you'll be obsessing on those instead of trying to figure out ways of how to solve them and you can either worry about a financial problem or you can set about solving it the problem is that that the worry keeps you from solving it because you're going to be obsessing and thinking negatively about all of the terrible things that could happen if you don't come up with a bunch of money instead of working hard and doing the things that's going to be necessary to bring in money to solve the problem so this is this is just the real nuts and bolts nitty gritty of the situation of the business there are dry spells you've got to pay yourself first and you know one of the things and a lot of people don't have a lot of financial discipline and I'm certainly infamous for that one I found having a safe deposit box or a or a even a storage box at MonEx or someplace like that and buying gold coins or you know socking cash away in it in other words make it hard to spend that money don't keep it in a checking account where you can just flash a debit card to start drawing it down make it a deliberate difficult decision to get to that money and and only use it if you absolutely have to to pay the pay the mortgage while you're while you're getting cash flow going again I agree another great way to do it get enough to put a deposit on a house and buy an investment property much you're much more likely to sell it but you're much more unlikely to just turn around and sell it and spend it you'll keep absolutely absolutely so we're almost at the end here let me we've got what I'd like to do is defer till next week which we're going to extend we're going to extend next week next week's coal which is the fourth in our series is the secret that items from this week uh the the uh number one productivity killer and the three mental tricks that kick its butt every time which we've sort of done but we'll review that next week some of this we've already covered a little but to to answer the last bullet of this week you promised us six ways to keep your creative juices flowing even when you think your brain is toast and how do you how do you because you can't always say I'm going to take a break or a day off sometimes even if you have a client who's understanding about deadlines sometimes the the deadlines are coming and you've got to do the work how do you how do you keep creative maybe when you're you're kind of at the end of your rope well I think we've covered most of these Bob let's let's want to go over them quickly sure well number one keep the momentum going um and and you can do that by as we talked about shifting from right to left brain tasks during the day the minute and it's like what one of the things I learned when riding a motorcycle over long distances is if you stop every hour your butt will hold up for the entire trip you can ride coast to coast right if your first hour on that bike you do two hours the rest of that trip you're going to be sore you know so before you reach the fatigue mark before you get to the point where you're where you're hurting where you've hit the wall you know shift off you know try to schedule your work so that you're shifting right to left brain the second one is the the idea of taking a 15 minute accounting break if you're working on creative tests in your block add up add up a column of numbers divide it by pie and do do and do it manually do something that involves fully involves the left side of the brain then return to it and I think in 15 minutes you can be back up and running again but again you have to do it early not after you hit the wall and have been struggling creatively for an hour or two that by then it's going to require more than just that 15 minute break but if you if you just work that in during the day it'll help the we talked about doing detailed detail oriented personal tasks once you checkbook pay the taxes do something like that that's another way to keep your productivity your creativity going we talked about mowing the lawn doing some something you enjoy doing that allows your brain to disengage and just kind of flirt with the work rather than really you know being fully engaged with it we talked about the napping approach and we talked about how sometimes all you can do is hang it up and when when you've tried everything else and you just can't get it and you don't have a detail oriented task to do then go ahead and hang it up and forgive yourself for it you know it's okay it's okay not to feel creative it's okay not to feel motivated you know with you when you've hit the wall everybody has those times but don't then go do something self-destructive some of the greatest writers ever like Hemingway for example the writing brought money money brought freedom freedom brought alcohol alcohol brought writer's block you know and and and then finally that ended very badly in a suicide i mean that was a self-destructive cycle where he was using unhealthy things to compensate for his inability to write or for his uh block creativity so you know keep that in mind as you're choosing what to do in the times when you're hanging it up and just walking away makes total sense and on that note we will end for today and next week we're going to do finish we're going to finish the couple of items from
